I believe this has been a good program, and I like that they ramped it up after the stock fell below what I’d consider their fair value, and their liquidity position remains strong to potentially continue returning significant cash to shareholders.

Iran Conflict Is A Double-Edged Sword

At this point, the Iran conflict can be a double-edged sword for a company like Dollar Tree, as the conflict can continue boosting their performance as they can attract more value-seeking customers, but it can also pressure their costs since we’re talking about a conflict that can lead to fundamental pressure across the broader market due to the region’s influence on vital commodities, spreading from oil and gas to fertilizers and plastics.

Still, although DLTR, Dollar General Corporation (DG), and the others have proven how much they can benefit from a high-rate environment with widespread consumer weakness, it’s important to note that they aren’t immune to a recession either, and the chances of that grow with every extra day in which the Strait of Hormuz remains closed, even though Iran proposed reopening the strait hours ago (with ​nuclear negotiations ​postponed for a ‌later ⁠stage).

At this point, we saw S&P Global already expect the Fed to delay rate cuts to 2027, while the agency and the IMF already expect lower GDP growth rates across the globe as a result. In other words, we’re likely to see an environment in which DLTR could benefit for longer, with more pressure on the consumer being very likely at this point, as the conflict goes well beyond just higher fuel prices.

This can further accentuate the K-shaped economic discrepancies and trade-downs that fueled growth in this segment over the past years, which likely also played a key role in the company's strong growth in sales per square foot since the pandemic.

Although this is a normal response to a high-rate environment, further pressure from tariffs and now Iran and its potential higher-for-longer interest rate scenario can lead to even more weakness in an already tough macro setting, and it’s no wonder why peers like DG are actually expanding lately, or why stores like Temu by PDD Holding Inc. (PDD) boomed a few years ago, or why Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) launched Amazon Haul in late 2024 and is aggressively expanding it. The company’s CEO also talked about this during their Q4’FY25 Earnings Call, stating:

Higher prices at the pump impact all households. And so what we see is in the middle to higher income households, we see accelerated trade-in. So those customers are turning towards Dollar Tree. And then our core customer, the lower-income shopper, our pack sizes are what really help them stretch their paycheck and make their budgets work for them. So the price impacts everyone, but for us, Dollar Tree is really that key tool that helps them manage their budgets and deal with these higher prices. And when you look back and see, I mentioned this in the press release, for 20 years, Dollar Tree has been posting positive comps. There's a lot of economic cycles in those 20 years. And the one constant is that Dollar Tree continues to be the answer across all income levels as people help live and celebrate their lives.

However, this type of competition combined with the rise of private label brands from retailers like Walmart (WMT) can also be a major threat to the dollar store segment, as it can lead to significant competitive pressure while costs are rising due to inflation caused by the previous tariffs, higher shipping costs, etc., forcing companies like DLTR into either limiting their margins or specializing in an effort to maintain their identity and differentiate from the giant competitors.

This is a major fundamental threat that can push the company and its peers into moves like continuing their “treasure hunt” focus, targeting rural areas where giants like Walmart wouldn’t step in, or even launching higher-cost products (e.g., Dollar Tree Plus) in an effort to attract more consumers.

Risks Reiterated

Meanwhile, although DLTR could benefit from a higher-for-longer rate environment, the company itself is not immune from it either, while private labels and the general competition from low-cost retailers can push value-seeking customers towards these in the longer term, making it tougher for them to capture these benefits over the longer term throughout the cycle.

Although the company is trying to adapt, streamlining their operations and focusing on a multi-pricing strategy, they are getting closer and closer to the same territory as the major retailers, fighting for a low-margin industry and risking being “left out” of the market as these giants focus on keeping clients inside their ecosystem.

Valuation

For Dollar Tree’s DCF to Equity valuation, starting from the cash flow reported last year and given the guidance while normalizing it to take out the net operating loss tax gain they expect this year (a one-time tax saving related to the Family Dollar era), I’ll start with a $1.15 billion FCF in FY26. Given their significant growth phase, potential for the CapEx to cool down, their ability to benefit from a weaker-for-longer environment, and also the major buybacks boosting the final per-share effect, let’s assume an 8% CAGR over the first 5 years (below the market’s EPS expectations, for comparison), with a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate.

However, given the macro landscape and the rising risks, I’m going to use a 10% discount rate, with 199.8 million shares outstanding as of their Q4 report.

This gets us to an equity value of $20.40 billion, for an intrinsic value of $102.12 per share, which is nearly identical to the current $103.75 at the moment of writing this, indicating that the market is already using a solid margin of safety despite the company’s potential pivot now, unlike the $133.78 seen when I last covered them.

Conclusions

Dollar Tree is upgraded to Buy, as the valuation now reflects a much more appropriate margin of safety while the company stands to benefit from a weaker environment for longer and is working on its pivot.

There are risks to consider, especially over the longer term, given the competitive pressure, but the company’s multi-price strategy and ability to benefit from the weaker-for-longer environment and the level of risk that’s already priced in justify the rating.

data.title: Intel: Keep Your SOX On
data.description:
Since my last Intel Corporation (INTC) analysis, in which I issued valuation caution, the stock has surged by a further 30% already. That's not a reason to turn bullish and may reflect momentum factors that are detached from fundamentals. Nonetheless, the business is starting to show signs of its thundering operational comeback, proving enduring, with recent Q1 2026 results revealing how so.

We're clearly past the narrative question of whether Intel can survive and into the question of whether Intel can produce enough. In the earnings call, management said demand still outruns supply across businesses, and AI-driven businesses are 60% of revenue and grew by 40% year-over-year. The data is no longer trying to prove relevance, which was the case just 12 months ago.

Powerfully, Intel doesn't have to beat Nvidia (NVDA) to win as a company. While Intel's DCAI segment saw revenue up 22% year-over-year in Q1, the company is monetizing CPU orchestration as AI moves to inference and agents. This differs significantly from Nvidia's GPU-dominant foundation. Intel's management said that CPU/GPU ratios moved from about 1:8 in training to about 1:4 in inference, and that we could see parity as agents proliferate.

And we should remember that Intel remains a well-diversified business. While this may reduce torque in exposure to the AI-CPU trade, it does add a layer of operational security. That said, because the valuation is rallying ahead of fundamentals, this does create the risk of the equity being mispriced by the market as an AI pure-play, which obviously it isn't.

18A, which has been in the pipeline for what feels like a long time now, is acting as a de-risking catalyst for the market. Intel 18A-based Core Series 3 is joint in being Intel's fastest new product ramp in five years, alongside Intel 3-based Xeon 6.

Foundry, recently viewed as "optionality" and a lagging aspect of Intel's broader strategy, showed promise in the form of internal leverage. Revenue from the segment was up by 16% year-over-year. Yields improved across Intel 4, 3, and 18A. And so there's an emerging future growth catalyst here of 18A moving from a present margin headwind to a tailwind as wafer outputs rise.

Finally, we had partner validation push the stock to all-time highs. Google (GOOG, GOOGL) LTAs, Xeon 6 in Nvidia DGX Rubin NVL8, SambaNova inference work, and SpaceX/xAI/Tesla (TSLA) Terafab engagement all signal that Intel is back in the group of high-end AI customers. This is a win.

Valuation

I will get onto the broader points about risks after this section, but first, we should discuss the elephant in the room. Intel stock is currently overextended on weekly RSI, now reading 83.2 as I write this. The stock is at all-time highs after being priced around 2009 levels just 12 months prior. This is either the start of a new Nvidia-style run or the peak of excess sentiment.

I would argue a crash isn't on the cards, but that the period of explosive growth is over and we are likely to see waves of moderate pullbacks in the medium-term trend of ~30% EPS CAGR. This means the present entry point is full of volatility risk, and that the 30% CAGR likely won't convert to direct price returns, given the forward P/E is higher than it should be. Notably, however, INTC's non-GAAP PEG is 30% below the broader semiconductor industry median, so the stock is not so expensive that holding on is unwise.

We can also look at the investment next to the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index (SOX), and we can see that this exuberance is not isolated to INTC alone. My perspective is that one may wish to trim here, but I'd keep SOX and INTC in your portfolio if you bought them at earlier, cheaper valuations. The compounding trajectory remains robust, and this is not a short-term cyclical wave. It is at worst a series of cyclical waves in a large megatrend to trim into strength and add into weakness, and at best it is one largely uninterrupted tsunami wave of growth with only marginal consolidation and cooling-off periods, much like we have seen so far with NVDA.

I predict multiple compressions for INTC in the base case, but they may not be severe given the present forward PEG optics. If the company achieves April 2028 normalized EPS of $1.75 and the stock has a forward P/E of 60 in April 2027, then the stock price will be $105 in about 12 months. The current price is $84, so the implied upside is 25%. Due to volatility risk and the potential for deeper multiple compression, I rate INTC a Hold now. But I congratulate management on an amazing turnaround and think the business will continue to thrive.

Risks

For comparison, we should put Intel's valuation in perspective with other leading semiconductor peers, as this will help us to evaluate the risk profile of INTC stock. As we can see in the following chart, the forward P/E for INTC is the highest against Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) and NVDA, and in fact almost triple that of NVDA. This compares to a long-term EPS CAGR consensus estimate of 73% for INTC and 38% for NVDA. In this scenario, I would say NVDA is undervalued, and INTC is overvalued. In other words, NVDA is currently the better investment.

Even though Intel Foundry is now showing more promise, it's still largely unproven commercially, with revenue of only $174M (after Altera deconsolidation). The 10-Q even says that right now, substantially all Foundry activity supports Intel Products, meaning that the initial plan to market the Foundry to high-level Western AI peers has not yet borne fruit.

On a final note, margin upside will not be linear, and Intel is operating in a free cash flow and capital-intensive trajectory. The Q2 non-GAAP gross margin guide has fallen to 39% from 41% as the 18A mix rises and Q1 inventory benefits fade. It's logical that the market has become over-exuberant here and is underestimating how costly a successful ramp for Intel will be. That thesis aligns with the valuation multiples we are seeing, outlined in the chart above.

Conclusion: Hold

By all means, hold on for the ride, but if your whole portfolio is your entire outfit, semiconductor stocks like Intel should probably be akin to your socks. They'll help you get to where you're going, but at this valuation, they certainly aren't your main draw. That's why I say keep your SOX on, but don't overweight an expensive sector that in many respects should be due for a correction soon. If one doesn't occur, a period of consolidation at least could lead to better time-value on your money to increase position sizing later.

Intel is performing turnaround miracles, it seems, but the markets can turn good news into a too-high stock price. The price resurgence was warranted, but the overall price of the company now is wrong. An overvalued equity does not mean it is bad to own, but it does mean it is unwise to buy. Expect some volatility ahead, but use any dislocation or consolidation window as your "add zones." Buying into a dramatic spike like this has rarely been a winner's strategy when the fundamentals also spell "overvalued."
